perturbation.original_text: Recipient shall follow U.S. Ex port Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products rece ived from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient shall follow U.S. Ex port Administration laws and regulations and shall not export or re-export any technical data or products rece ived from Discloser or the direct product of such technical data to any proscribed country listed in the U.S. Export Administration regulations unless properly authorized by the U.S. government.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the export compliance clause to the 'Disclaimer' section may obscure its importance and create ambiguity regarding its enforceability, potentially violating export control regulations.
perturbation.law_explanation: Export Administration Regulations require clear compliance measures. Embedding this in a disclaimer undermines its visibility and enforceability, contradicting the intent of the EAR.

Skip to main content Export Administration Regulations (EAR) This tool helps you navigate the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Commerce Control List (CCL) The Commerce Control List (CCL) (Supplement No. 1 to Part 774 of the EAR) is divided into ten broad categories, and each category is further subdivided into five product groups. Additionally, you may explore our interactive tool . Category 0 - Nuclear Materials Facilities & Equipment [and Miscellaneous Items] Category 1 - Special Materials and Related Equipment, Chemicals, “Microorganisms,” and “Toxins” Category 2 - Materials Processing Category 3 - Electronics Category 4 - Computers Category 5 Part 1 - Telecommunications Category 5 Part 2 - Information Security Category 6 - Sensors and Lasers Category 7 - Navigation and Avionics Category 8 - Marine Category 9 - Aerospace and Propulsion Legal limitations of the tools on the BIS website The tools on the BIS website are not intended to replace the official rules found in the Code of Federal Regulations , nor are they a primer on how to get started. For that introductory guidance, please visit our Learn and Support page. The tools are an editorial compilation of material and not an official legal edition of the Code of Federal Regulations or the Federal Register . We have made every effort to ensure the material presented in these tools are accurate, but if you are relying on them for legal research you should consult the official editions of those sources to confirm your findings. Nothing in these tools binds BIS or creates any rights, benefits, or defenses, substantive or procedural, that are enforceable by any party in any manner. If you find any errors, please email us.
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the enforceability of the export compliance clause by potentially obscuring its importance within a disclaimer section. This contradicts the intent of Export Administration Regulations (EAR), which require clear compliance measures, as stated in the Law Explanation. While the scraped snippets don't explicitly forbid placing compliance clauses in disclaimers, the 'EAR | Bureau of Industry and Security' snippet emphasizes navigating the Export Administration Regulations (EAR) under 15 CFR 730-774. Embedding the clause in a less visible section undermines the visibility and enforceability, thus creating a Structural Flaw, and a Legal Contradiction because the clarity/enforceability of the clause is affected by its placement in the contract. This aligns with the Structural Flaws - Legal Contradiction definition: 'Structural flaws occur when the organization of a contract affects its clarity or enforceability. A legal contradiction in this category arises when a required legal disclosure is placed in an irrelevant or misleading section, making it difficult for parties to locate or interpret.'

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in ac cordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions).
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in ac cordance with the laws of Utah (without regard to conflicts of laws provisions).
perturbation.explanation: Moving the governing law clause to Section 8 'Disclaimer' may create uncertainty and weaken its enforceability, as disclaimers are typically related to limiting liability rather than establishing governing law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, Section 187 of the Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws is widely influential. Moving the governing law clause to the 'Disclaimer' section suggests it's a limitation rather than a firm selection of Utah law, creating ambiguity about the parties' intent, potentially contradicting the principles of contract interpretation.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The perturbation explanation suggests that relocating the governing law clause to the 'Disclaimer' section introduces ambiguity regarding its enforceability, which aligns with the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition. The law explanation reinforces this, pointing out that such placement might imply a limitation rather than a firm selection of Utah law. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'conflict of laws' as a difference between jurisdictions and emphasizes the importance of determining which law to apply. By moving the clause to a less prominent section, the contract weakens the clear selection of Utah law, potentially leading to disputes over which jurisdiction's law governs, thus creating a legal contradiction by undermining the intended certainty and enforceability of the governing law provision.
